1: paqudat ha'ir ('those charged with the punishment of the city'): paqudat (oversight/charge/punishment) — the root paqad is both 'to visit' (in care) and 'to visit' (in judgment). Six executioners approach from the north — the direction from which Babylon will come. The summoning of divine executioners frames the coming slaughter not as human atrocity but as divinely authorized judgment.
2: keli mashchet beyado ('a weapon of destruction in his hand'): each executioner carries keli mashchet — an instrument of shattering/destruction. The one exception is the man levush habadim ('dressed in linen'): priestly linen (bad) marks him as a different order of being — a priestly-scribal figure who marks the saved, not executes the judged. qeset hasopher ('writing case of the scribe'): an ink-holder for marking.
3: ukhvod Elohei Yisrael na'alah ('the glory of the God of Israel went up'): the glory begins its departure. From the kerub (cherub) on which it rested — its traditional seat above the ark in the Holy of Holies — it moves to the threshold of the temple. This is the first stage of the glory's withdrawal that will culminate in 10:18-19 and 11:23. The departure of the glory is the most theologically catastrophic event in the entire book.
4: vesimta tav al-mitzchotam ('put a mark on the foreheads'): tav (תָּו) is the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet, and in paleo-Hebrew script was written as an X or + shape. The mark identifies those who ne'enachim v'ne'anaqim ('sigh and groan') over all the abominations done in Jerusalem. The criterion for salvation is not ritual performance but grief-identification with YHWH's perspective on the apostasy. Those who mourn over sin are marked as those who share divine values.
5: acharav amar b'oznai ('to the others he said in my hearing'): Ezekiel hears the divine command to the executioners, not merely as a vision but as audible speech in his ears. The command to follow the scribe through the city mirrors the Exodus: the blood on the doorposts marked the saved; here the ink-mark on the forehead marks those who are passed over. The executioners begin with the elders before the sanctuary — judgment begins at the house of God (cf. 1 Pet 4:17).
6: el-zaqen bachur u'vetulah vetaf u'nashim harogu lemashchit ('old man, young man, maiden, infant, and women — kill to destruction'): the totalistic list — no age or sex is exempt from the judgment. The sole exception: veal-kol-ish asher-alav hatav al-tiqrabu ('do not come near any man on whom is the mark'). Ubeit hamiqdash tachelu ('from my sanctuary you shall begin'): judgment starting in the temple — the most horrifying reversal, since the temple was supposed to be the place of refuge.
7: tumeu et-habayit ('defile the house'): the executioners are commanded to defile the temple — the very space that was supposed to be the holiest place in Israel is made a slaughterhouse. The chatzerot mil'u chalal ('fill the courts with the slain') creates a spatial horror: the courts for sacrificial worship become the courts of corpses. YHWH himself commands the defilement of his own house as the final statement of judgment on Israel's apostasy.
8: veten Ezekiel el-panav ('and I fell on my face'): Ezekiel's intercession — the same posture of pleading as Moses (Num 14:5; 16:22). ha'atah meshalecheta et kol she'erit Yisrael ('are you going to destroy all the remnant of Israel?'): the prophet's one intercessory cry in the midst of the vision. Unlike Jeremiah (7:16; 11:14), Ezekiel is not explicitly told not to intercede, but the answer he receives makes clear that the judgment cannot be arrested.
9: avon beit Yisrael uYehudah gadol meod meod ('the iniquity of the house of Israel and Judah is very, very great'): the double intensifier meod meod (very, very) drives home the disproportionality — this is not slight unfaithfulness but massive, accumulated, comprehensive covenant violation. YHWH names two specific theological failures: (1) the land is full of blood (dam); (2) the city is full of perversion (mateh). Both are covenant violations with capital consequences in Torah.
10: gam ani lo-tachus einai velo echmol ('I also — my eye will not spare and I will not pity'): the formula from 7:4,9 and 8:18 now from YHWH's own lips in direct speech to the interceding Ezekiel. The 'I also' (gam ani) is devastating in its context: Ezekiel has just shown compassion ('will you destroy the remnant?'); YHWH responds with the divine reciprocity of derakham beroshham natati — 'their way I repay on their heads.'
11: ani asiti ka'asher tzivitani ('I have done as you commanded me'): the linen-clad scribe reports completion. The chapter ends on the note of the one mission accomplished: the mark has been placed. Against the total devastation of the city, the marking of the remnant is the sole exception. The divine plan that preserves a remnant while executing judgment on the apostate majority is achieved with precise obedience.
